message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 10
Recent changes: Added Customer type and loyalty >= 2 branch returning hardcoded 93

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll run the refactor phase for the latest changes. Let me start by reading the relevant files.

content.content:
architecture-notes.md
eslint.config.mjs
example-mapping
metrics.json
node_modules
package.json
pnpm-lock.yaml
prompt.md
run.log
src
tdd-journal.md
tsconfig.json
vitest.config.ts

1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- Change: introduced types `ScenarioResult = { premium?: number; payout?: number }` and `ScenarioOutput = { results: ScenarioResult[] }`; typed `runScenario` return as `ScenarioOutput`
5	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — make the output contract explicit at the type level so future cycles have a shared shape for both quote (`premium`) and claim (`payout`) results
6	- Naming established: `runScenario(input): ScenarioOutput`; result entries use keys `premium` and `payout`
7	
8	## After cycle 2
9	- Change: replaced `itemType === "amulet" ? 71 : 115` with a `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE` lookup map (`{ sword: 115, amulet: 71 }`)
10	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — both known item premiums are now equally enumerated rather than one being an implicit ternary fallback; also lowers APP mass (13 → 11) by removing the conditional. Map will naturally extend as upcoming staff/potion/rune/moonstone tests land
11	- Naming established: module-level `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE: Record<string, number>` as the per-item-type premium lookup
12	
13	## After cycle 3
14	- Change: inlined the local `premium` binding in `runScenario` into the returned object literal (`{ premium: PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE[itemType] }`); also added `staff: 93` to the lookup map (this was the Green change)
15	- Rationale: Rule 4 (fewest elements) — the named binding was redundant because the object key `premium:` already self-documents the value; mass −1. Kept `itemType` binding because `steps[0].items[0].type` is about to be reused once surcharges/discounts land
16	- Naming established: —
17	
18	## After cycle 4
19	- No refactoring (justified): Green only added `potion: 49` to `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`. Tried destructuring `steps[0]`/`items[0]` into `firstStep`/`firstItem` bindings but reverted — added two bindings with no clarity gain over the direct `steps[0].items[0].type` access. Extracting the implicit `base * 1.1 + 5` formula behind the cached premiums would be premature: upcoming loyalty-discount tests will replace the `* 1.1` first-contract surcharge, and only then will the fee/surcharge split become observable. Tightening `Step.op: string` to a literal union would speculate on the claim-op shape. Function stays at ~8 mass, fully justified by 4 passing tests.
20	
21	## After cycle 5
22	- No refactoring (justified): Green added `rune: 33` to `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`. Rune is the first test where rounding becomes implicit (25 * 1.1 = 27.5 → ceil → 28, then +5 = 33), giving the first weak signal that a `Math.ceil(base * 1.1) + 5` formula extraction with a `BASE_BY_ITEM_TYPE` map is coming. Holding the extraction because: (a) `Math.ceil`, `Math.round`, and `Math.trunc+1` all satisfy this lone non-integer case — no test yet forces ceil specifically; (b) the dedicated "round UP in MHPCO's favor" todo (staff + loyalty + first = 76G from 70.4) will force ceil unambiguously and will land alongside the loyalty discount, at which point extracting base+formula+rounding together is one coherent change instead of two speculative ones. Naming/binding/element review unchanged from cycle 4.
23	
24	## After cycle 6
25	- Change: extracted `const COMPONENT_PREMIUM = 33` and referenced it from both `rune` and `moonstone` entries in `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`
26	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) + partial Rule 3 (literal duplication) — moonstone is the second 33G entry, and the spec calls rune/moonstone "components" sharing base 25G. Naming the shared value documents the category rather than leaving it a coincidence of two equal literals. Anticipates upcoming component-block tests (lines 96-100) that will distinguish component pricing further. Module-level mass +1; accepted because Rule 2 trumps APP. Still NOT extracting `Math.ceil(base * 1.1) + 5` + `BASE_BY_ITEM_TYPE` — cycle 5 reasoning unchanged (ceil not yet forced; loyalty/fee split not yet observable).
27	- Naming established: module-level `COMPONENT_PREMIUM: number` as the shared per-unit premium for component items (rune, moonstone, …)
28	
29	## After cycle 7
30	- No refactoring (justified): Green added a literal `if (item.cursed) return { results: [{ premium: 170 }] }` branch and a small `item = steps[0].items[0]` binding (reused twice). Evaluated three Rule-2 candidates and rejected all: (a) replacing `170` with `100 * 1.5 * 1.1 + 5` matches the spec phrasing but adds ~+9 mass for a one-off intermediate-value reveal that will be obsoleted next cycle by the highly-enchanted (148G) and loyalty (93G) sword tests, which together force a `Math.ceil(base * surcharges * 1.1) + 5` extraction with named multipliers — doing it now would be the speculative two-step path cycle 5 warned against; (b) introducing `CURSED_SWORD_PREMIUM = 170` just relabels the magic number without tying it to the sword base of 115 already in the lookup — no Rule 3 win since `cursed` is per-item not per-type so it can't fold into `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`; (c) replacing `170` with `Math.ceil(100 * 1.5 * 1.1) + 5` is the same speculation as (a) plus a ceil that this integer test doesn't force. The cursed branch is intentionally parked as a hardcoded-return placeholder, mirroring how per-type premiums sat as bare numbers in cycles 1-2 before `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE` emerged from accumulated signal. Extraction will happen when the surcharge family (highly-enchanted, loyalty, first-contract, stacking, round-up-staff) has landed enough tests to drive one coherent `applyMultipliers` + base-lookup + ceil refactor. Naming on `item`, `runScenario`, `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`, `COMPONENT_PREMIUM` all still accurate.
31	
32	## After cycle 8
33	- Change: extracted `const HIGHLY_ENCHANTED_MIN = 5` at module scope and replaced the literal `5` in the `item.enchantment >= 5` guard with the named binding
34	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the spec uses the phrase "highly enchanted" and the very next test (line 118, enchantment=4 boundary => stays at 115G) makes this threshold semantically load-bearing. Naming the magic number documents which side of the boundary is "highly enchanted" before that test forces the discussion. Mirrors cycle 6's `COMPONENT_PREMIUM` extraction — name the threshold the spec already names. Module mass +1 (new binding); function body mass unchanged (literal 5 → binding ref, both score 1). Accepted because Rule 2 trumps APP. Still NOT extracting the formula/`applyMultipliers` helper or replacing the hardcoded 170/148 with `Math.ceil(base * multipliers * 1.1) + 5`: cycle 7's reasoning still holds — only two surcharges are active and both are single-multiplier single-item integer cases; loyalty discount, first-contract surcharge, ceil rounding (round-up-staff test), and stacking (cursed+enchanted=220G) are all still unforced. The two parallel hardcoded branches (170, 148) will be replaced together by the same coherent refactor once the surcharge family lands enough signal.
35	- Naming established: module-level `HIGHLY_ENCHANTED_MIN: number` as the inclusive enchantment threshold at which an item counts as "highly enchanted" for quote surcharging (and, anticipated, claim eligibility)
36	
37	## After cycle 9
38	- Change: extracted `const isHighlyEnchanted = (item: Item): boolean => (item.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGHLY_ENCHANTED_MIN` at module scope; the `if` guard at the surcharge branch is now `if (isHighlyEnchanted(item))`
39	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — Green for cycle 9 was a no-op (enchantment=4 boundary already passed), so the only refactoring signal comes from naming. The cursed branch reads as `if (item.cursed)` — a categorical test. The enchantment branch was the only one mixing nullish coalescing with arithmetic, reading as a numeric comparison rather than a category check. The predicate `isHighlyEnchanted` mirrors the spec phrase (and the threshold name from cycle 8) and makes both surcharge guards read as parallel category tests. Directly anticipates the upcoming stacking test ("cursed AND highly enchanted = 220G", line 140) where `if (item.cursed && isHighlyEnchanted(item))` will read symmetrically. Mass: +1 binding (the arrow fn), +1 invocation at call site, -1 invocation (the `>=` is now inside the helper, neutral overall on conditional body); net ~+2. Accepted because Rule 2 trumps APP. Still NOT extracting the 170/148 formula or a base-lookup — cycles 5/7/8 reasoning unchanged: ceil-forcing test (round-up-staff with loyalty), loyalty discount, first-contract split, and surcharge stacking all still unforced; doing the formula extraction now would speculate on which factors multiply (base * 1.5 * 1.1 vs base * (1 + 0.5) * 1.1, with-or-without ceil, etc.). The 170 and 148 stay parked together until the surcharge family lands.
40	- Naming established: module-level `isHighlyEnchanted(item: Item): boolean` as the categorical predicate for the highly-enchanted surcharge guard (and, anticipated, claim eligibility — the claim section spec uses the same "highly enchanted" phrase at lines 149-151)
41

content.text: Now let me run the tests to confirm green state, then evaluate refactoring.

content.text:
All 10 tests pass. Now let me evaluate the refactor decision.

## Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

Current names in `runScenario`:
- `runScenario` — accurate; still drives a scenario
- `item`, `customer`, `steps` — accurate destructured names
- `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`, `COMPONENT_PREMIUM`, `HIGHLY_ENCHANTED_MIN`, `isHighlyEnchanted` — all accurate from prior cycles
- The new loyalty branch uses `(customer?.yearsWithMHPCO ?? 0) >= 2` — uses bare literal `2`

The literal `2` has no name. The spec phrase is "loyalty discount" and the threshold is the inclusive years count. Naming candidate: `LOYALTY_MIN_YEARS = 2`. This mirrors cycle 8's exact pattern (`HIGHLY_ENCHANTED_MIN`) and the upcoming boundary test (line 146: "should NOT apply the loyalty discount when yearsWithMHPCO=1") makes this threshold semantically load-bearing — just like the enchantment=4 boundary did for `HIGHLY_ENCHANTED_MIN`.

Additionally, parallel to cycle 9's `isHighlyEnchanted`, the loyalty branch reads as a numeric comparison rather than a category check. A predicate `hasLoyalty(customer)` would make all three guards read as parallel category tests:
- `if (item.cursed)`
- `if (isHighlyEnchanted(item))`
- `if (hasLoyalty(customer))`

## Current Code Mass

```typescript
if ((customer?.yearsWithMHPCO ?? 0) >= 2) {
  return { results: [{ premium: 93 }] };
}
```

Components for this branch only:
- Constants: `0`, `2`, `93` = 3
- Bindings: `customer` ref = 1
- Invocations: `?.`, `??`, `>=` ~= 3 → 6
- Conditional: 1 → 4
- Object/array literal: ~0
- Subtotal: ~14

## Refactoring Decision

I'll apply two parallel-with-prior-cycles changes:
1. Extract `LOYALTY_MIN_YEARS = 2` (mirrors cycle 8's `HIGHLY_ENCHANTED_MIN`)
2. Extract `hasLoyalty(customer)` predicate (mirrors cycle 9's `isHighlyEnchanted`)

This makes all three surcharge/discount guards read symmetrically and prepares for the upcoming stacking test ("cursed + highly enchanted + loyalty + first" = 177G, line 154) where the predicates will combine. This is NOT speculative formula extraction — the hardcoded 93 stays parked alongside 170 and 148 until the surcharge family lands.

content.text:
Tests stay green (10 passing, 28 todo). The task list is owned by the orchestrator across phases, so no update needed from the refactor agent (the orchestrator will mark test 10 complete after this phase returns).

## Refactored Code Mass

For the loyalty branch + the new helper:

Module additions:
- `LOYALTY_MIN_YEARS`: 1 binding + 1 constant = 2
- `hasLoyalty`: 1 binding (the arrow fn) + ~5 inside body (parameter ref, `?.`, `??`, `>=`, `0`, `customer`, `yearsWithMHPCO`) ≈ 7

In-function loyalty branch:
- Bindings: `customer` ref = 1
- Invocations: `hasLoyalty(...)` = 2
- Conditional: 1 → 4
- Constants: `93` = 1
- Subtotal: ~8

Total for loyalty-related changes: previous ~14 in-branch → ~8 in-branch + ~9 module = net **slightly higher mass (~+3)**.

**Trade-off accepted**: Rule 2 (reveals intent) trumps APP. The three guard predicates now read as parallel category tests, the threshold `2` is named, and the upcoming boundary test (yearsWithMHPCO=1) will read against a named threshold.

## Documentation

input.command:
cat >> /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_19-50-14_claim-office-lite-prose_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/architecture-notes.md << 'EOF'

## After cycle 10
- Change: extracted `const LOYALTY_MIN_YEARS = 2` and `const hasLoyalty = (customer: Customer | undefined): boolean => (customer?.yearsWithMHPCO ?? 0) >= LOYALTY_MIN_YEARS` at module scope; the loyalty guard in `runScenario` is now `if (hasLoyalty(customer))`
- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — twofold mirror of the threshold/predicate pattern established in cycles 8 and 9. (a) `LOYALTY_MIN_YEARS` parallels `HIGHLY_ENCHANTED_MIN`: the spec phrase "loyalty discount" and the very next test (line 146, yearsWithMHPCO=1 boundary => stays at 115G) make this threshold semantically load-bearing, exactly as the enchantment=4 test did for `HIGHLY_ENCHANTED_MIN`. (b) `hasLoyalty` parallels `isHighlyEnchanted`: the three guard branches in `runScenario` now read as symmetric category tests — `if (item.cursed)`, `if (isHighlyEnchanted(item))`, `if (hasLoyalty(customer))` — instead of the loyalty branch standing out as a numeric comparison with nullish coalescing. Directly anticipates line 154 ("cursed + highly enchanted + loyalty + first" = 177G) where the three predicates will combine in one boolean expression. Mass: +2 (constant + binding) at module scope, +~7 for `hasLoyalty` body, -~6 in `runScenario` body (the `?.`/`??`/`>=`/`0` collapse to one `hasLoyalty(customer)` invocation); net ~+3 module-wide. Accepted because Rule 2 trumps APP. Still NOT extracting the 170/148/93 formula or a base-lookup — cycles 5/7/8/9 reasoning unchanged: ceil-forcing test (round-up-staff with loyalty, line 156), first-contract surcharge split, and stacking (cursed+enchanted=220G line 153; all four modifiers=177G line 154) are all still unforced. The three hardcoded branches (170, 148, 93) stay parked together — they will collapse into one `Math.ceil(base * surchargeProduct * 1.1) + 5` formula once the surcharge family has landed enough signal to lock in which factors multiply and the ceil semantics.
- Naming established: module-level `LOYALTY_MIN_YEARS: number` as the inclusive yearsWithMHPCO threshold at which the 20% loyalty discount applies; module-level `hasLoyalty(customer: Customer | undefined): boolean` as the categorical predicate for the loyalty-discount guard
EOF

content.text:
## Refactor Phase Complete

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Extracted `LOYALTY_MIN_YEARS = 2` module constant (mirrors cycle 8's `HIGHLY_ENCHANTED_MIN`)
- Extracted `hasLoyalty(customer)` predicate (mirrors cycle 9's `isHighlyEnchanted`)
- Loyalty guard now reads `if (hasLoyalty(customer))`, parallel to the cursed/highly-enchanted guards

**Mass Change**: net ~+3 module-wide; in-function branch reduced from ~14 to ~8. Accepted because Rule 2 (reveals intent) trumps APP.

**Tests**: All 10 passing, 28 todo.

**Why not the formula extraction (still parked)**: The three hardcoded premium branches (170, 148, 93) will collapse together into one `Math.ceil(base * surchargeProduct * 1.1) + 5` formula once the surcharge family has landed: ceil-forcing test (line 156), first-contract surcharge split, and stacking tests (lines 153-154). Extracting now would speculate on factor structure.

Proceeding to the next test (test 11: loyalty boundary yearsWithMHPCO=1 stays at 115G).
